memes.label: Angel (Imagery)
memes.description: The angel as a figure for the beloved's transformative presence in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — a being who mediates between heaven and earth, the divine and the human.

memes.label: Browning Courtship (1845–1846)
memes.description: The courtship and elopement of Elizabeth Barrett and Robert Browning (1845–1846), the biographical event that occasioned *Sonnets from the Portuguese*.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept pages in Sonnets from the Portuguese wiki, grouped by subtype.

memes.label: Death (Theme)
memes.description: The theme of death in *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, explored as a foil to love, a shadow of past grief, and the ultimate threshold beyond which love transcends.

memes.label: Dream (Motif)
memes.description: The dream and vision motif in Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese* contrasts the speaker's former life of solitary visions with the superior reality of the beloved's presence — dreams are revealed as pale anticipations of a love that surpasses imagination itself.

memes.label: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
memes.description: English poet (1806–1861), author of *Sonnets from the Portuguese* (1850), one of the most prominent Victorian poets whose courtship with Robert Browning produced the sequence's autobiographical love sonnets.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Index of event pages in the Sonnets from the Portuguese wiki.

memes.label: God
memes.description: God as a divine figure referenced throughout Elizabeth Barrett Browning's Sonnets from the Portuguese, appearing in over a dozen sonnets as witness, judge, creator, and object of faith.

memes.label: Grief (Theme)
memes.description: Grief as love's inseparable companion in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — the accumulated sorrow of the speaker's past that love acknowledges, transforms, but never erases.

memes.label: Heart (Imagery)
memes.description: The heart as the embodied center of emotional life in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — heavy with grief, lifted by love, and the organ through which the speaker most directly registers love's physical reality.

memes.label: Imagery Index
memes.description: Index of all imagery-related concept pages in the Sonnets from the Portuguese wiki.

memes.label: Kiss (Imagery)
memes.description: The kiss as the physical sacrament of love in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — the embodied act that seals the spiritual union with tangible, mortal presence.

memes.label: Light (Imagery)
memes.description: Light as the dominant metaphor for love's transformative illumination in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — the beloved's presence that dispels the speaker's former darkness.

memes.label: Love (Theme)
memes.description: The central theme of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, explored as spiritual devotion, romantic passion, grief, and transcendent union across all 44 sonnets.

relations.label: Sonnet XLIII — "How do I love thee?"

memes.label: Music (Theme)
memes.description: Music in Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese* serves as a metaphor for the speaker's poetic voice, her emotional register (melancholy, sweetness), and the transcendent harmony of divine love.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person pages in Sonnets from the Portuguese wiki.

memes.label: Petrarchan Sonnet Form
memes.description: The fourteen-line Italian poetic form (ABBA ABBA CDC DCD) that structures *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, adapted by Elizabeth Barrett Browning to give voice to a female speaker's experience of reciprocal love.

memes.label: Religious Faith
memes.description: The religious and theological dimension of *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, in which God appears as witness, benefactor, and ultimate reference point for love's meaning.

memes.label: Robert Browning
memes.description: English poet (1812–1889), husband of Elizabeth Barrett Browning and the addressee of *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, a major Victorian poet in his own right known for dramatic monologues.

memes.label: Shadow (Motif)
memes.description: The shadow and shade motif in Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese* spans the darkness of grief, the protective shadow of the beloved's presence, and the shadow as poetic shelter — evolving from a marker of melancholy to a sign of intimate union.

memes.label: Silence (Theme)
memes.description: Silence in Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese* is not absence but a form of presence — a spiritual language that surpasses speech, a sanctuary for intimate union, and the condition in which the deepest love is expressed without words.

memes.label: Sonnet I ("I thought once how Theocritus had sung")
memes.description: The opening sonnet of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's Sonnets from the Portuguese, introducing the sequence's central transformation from death to love through the figure of Theocritus and a mystical vision.

memes.label: Sonnet II ("But only three in all God's universe")
memes.description: Sonnet II of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846.

memes.label: Sonnet III ("Unlike are we, unlike, O princely Heart!")
memes.description: Sonnet III of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning.

memes.description: Complete index of all 44 sonnet work pages in Sonnets from the Portuguese wiki, grouped by quality tier.

memes.description: Sonnet VII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning.

memes.label: Sonnet VIII ("What can I give thee back, O liberal")
memes.description: Sonnet VIII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846.

memes.label: Sonnet X ("Yet, love, mere love, is beautiful indeed")
memes.description: Sonnet X of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846.

memes.label: Sonnet XI ("And therefore if to love can be desert,")
memes.description: Sonnet XI of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning.

memes.label: Sonnet XII ("Indeed this very love which is my boast")
memes.description: Sonnet XII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning. The speaker declares that even her greatest love is not her own achievement but derives entirely from the beloved's example, using the imagery of a ruby crown and a golden throne.

memes.label: Sonnet XIII ("And wilt thou have me fashion into speech")
memes.description: Sonnet XIII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. The speaker confronts the impossibility of expressing love in words, using the extended metaphor of a torch dropped at the beloved's feet and offering silent fortitude in place of speech.

memes.label: Sonnet XIV ("If thou must love me, let it be for nought")
memes.description: Sonnet XIV of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. One of the most quoted in the sequence, the speaker argues that love must be unconditional — not for smile, look, or pity — but purely for love's own sake, culminating in the imperative "love me for love's sake, that evermore / Thou mayst love on through love's eternity."

memes.label: Sonnet XIX ("The soul's Rialto hath its merchandise")
memes.description: Sonnet XIX of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. Reciprocating Sonnet XVIII's gift, the speaker receives a lock of the beloved's dark hair, comparing it to that praised by Pindar, and places it on her heart as a warming presence "till mine grows cold in death."

memes.label: Sonnet XLIII ("How Do I Love Thee?")
Sonnet XV ("Accuse me not, beseech thee, that I wear")
memes.description: Sonnet XV of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. The speaker explores the asymmetry of love — the beloved looks at her with untroubled devotion while she looks past love toward its ending, using the striking image of a bee shut in crystalline and the vista of rivers flowing to a bitter sea.

memes.label: Sonnet XVI ("And yet, because thou overcomest so")
memes.description: Sonnet XVI of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. A pivotal surrender sonnet in which the speaker finally accepts the beloved's love, using military and royal imagery to describe conquest not as humiliation but as elevation, culminating in the plea "Make thy love larger to enlarge my worth."

memes.label: Sonnet XVII ("My poet, thou canst touch on all the notes")
memes.description: Sonnet XVII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. Addressed to "My poet," the speaker asks the beloved — a fellow poet — what role she should play in his life, listing possibilities from hope to memory to shade to grave, culminating in the single word "Choose."

memes.label: Sonnet XVIII ("I never gave a lock of hair away")
memes.description: Sonnet XVIII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. The speaker gives the beloved a lock of her hair, a gift never before offered to any man. The lock bears the kiss her mother left when she died, making the gift a transmission of maternal love across generations.

memes.label: Sonnet XX ("Beloved, my Beloved, when I think")
memes.description: Sonnet XX of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. A meditation on the miracle of love's arrival — the speaker contrasts her past isolation ("alone here in the snow") with the wonder that the beloved existed in the world without her knowing, comparing atheists who cannot guess God's presence to those who would not sense love's approach.

memes.label: Sonnet XXI ("Say over again, and yet once over again")
memes.description: "Sonnet XXI of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846. The speaker begs the beloved to repeat 'thou dost love me' endlessly, comparing the repetition to the cuckoo's spring song. Each iteration is as welcome as the returning season. The sonnet closes with the paradox of loving in silence with the soul."

memes.label: Sonnet XXII ("When our two souls stand up erect and strong,")
memes.description: Sonnet XXII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning.

memes.label: Sonnet XXIII ("Is it indeed so? If I lay here dead")
memes.description: Sonnet XXIII of Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese*, composed 1845–1846.

memes.label: Sonnets from the Portuguese
memes.description: A sequence of 44 Petrarchan sonnets by Elizabeth Barrett Browning, written 1845–1846 during her courtship with Robert Browning and first published in 1850 as part of the revised *Poems*.

memes.label: Soul (Theme)
memes.description: The soul as the seat of love's deepest experience in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — the medium of spiritual union, the voice that speaks when the body cannot, and the eternal dimension that love claims for its own.

memes.label: Tear (Imagery)
memes.description: The tear as the physical manifestation of emotional intensity in *Sonnets from the Portuguese* — at once sign of grief and vehicle of purification.
